title: Febrile Paediatric (<12yo)
content_markdown:
#### Target Patient Cohort

- Age <12yo
- Temperature of >38.0\xb0C.

#### Assess

Clinical features suggestive of a compromised paediatric. 

Many clinical signs of compromise are subjective. Good practice is to be guided by parents and guardians. Do not discount parental concern.

##### Color

- Pallor
- Mottled skin
- Cyanosed
##### Activity

- Lethargy or decreased activity
- Abnormal response to social cues- Difficult to wake/does not stay awake- Weak, high-pitched or continuous cry
##### Respiratory

- Grunting
- Increased WOB- Hypoxia
##### Circulation and Hydration

- Poor feeding
- Dry mucous membranes- Central capillary refill ≥3 seconds- Reduced skin turgor- Reduced urine output
##### Neurological

- Bulging fontanelle
- Excessive irritability- Neck stiffness- Focal neurological signs- Focal, complex or prolonged seizures
##### Other

- Non-blanching rash
- Fever for ≥5 days- Swelling of a limb or joint- Non-weight bearing/not using an extremity

#### Differentiate

- **Compromised**
- Presence of clinical feature/s suggestive of compromise. 
**OR**
- Vital signs outside normal range for age, refer to Critical Care Calculator.

- **Uncompromised**
- Absence of clinical features suggestive of compromise. 
**AND**
- Vital signs within normal range for age, refer to Critical Care Calculator.

#### Compromised Mx

- Manage paediatrics with clinical signs of shock as per CMG38 Sepsis.
- Manage paediatrics presenting with decreased LOC within 12 hours of febrile illness onset as per CMG38a Meningococcal Disease.
- General supportive care

#### Uncompromised Mx

- Encourage oral fluid intake. Provide smaller amounts more frequently. 
- If the patient is <6 months and formula fed, offer their usual amount of formula.
- If the patient is <6 months and breastfed, offer additional breastfeeds.
- If the patient is >6 months keep breast/bottle feeding as usual. Can offer water or oral rehydration solutions as well.
- PO Paracetamol and Ibuprofen\xa0 

Paracetamol and Ibuprofen are only indicated to alleviate significant discomfort and should not be administered routinely for the sole purpose of reducing temperature.

There is no evidence to show Paracetamol or Ibuprofen reduce the liklihood or duration of febrile seizures.

- Bathing/showering in cold water is not recommended.
##### Differentiate Care Pathway

- GP or consider Walk-in-Centre. 
- Fever duration >24h and <5 days 
 or 
- Obvious clinical cause of infection.
- **Care at home.**\xa0 

Advice to guardian/s on how to care for the patient at home, and red flags indicating deterioration must be provided. If you think that advise has not been fully comprehended, consider guardian transport to ED, referral to WiC or GP.

Advice for guardian/s – Febrile Paediatrics Fact Sheet from RCH Melbourne

- Fever duration <24h and no obvious clinical cause of infection.
- Fever post vaccination. 

It’s common for a benign fever to start within 24h of vaccination, lasting for up to three days.

- **Transport**

Febrile paediatrics with any of the below should be transported to ED.

- <3 months corrected age. 

Calculation of a ‘corrected age’ is required for patients who were born prematurely. Subtract the amount of days the patient was born prematurely from their age to calculate the ‘corrected age’.

- Incomplete vaccinations.
- Immunosuppressed/oncological conditions.
- Congenital heart disease.
- Previous bacterial infections in the past month.
- Demographic risk factors. 

Certain demographics are at higher risk of unrecognised deterioration due to socio-determinants of health: Poor access or engagement with health services, poor diet, unhealthy living arrangements, limited access to clean drinking water, and higher rates of other chronic illnesses should be considered as part of a comprehensive assessment.

#### Note: Febrile Seizures

Febrile seizures are typically benign but can be complex requiring further investigation. Additionally, while a febrile seizure may be benign, the underlying febrile illness may not be. Transport to hospital is preferred, but it is permissible to leave the patient at home in the care of a responsible guardian and if the below criteria are met.

- > 6 months and < 6 years old.
- Uncompromised as per “Febrile Paediatric” differentiation.
- Seizure was generalised tonic-clonic in nature.
- Seizure duration was <15 /60. 

Any seizure with a duration ≥5/60 with associated decreased LOC should be treated as status epilepticus as per CMG 22 Seizures.

- Complete recovery to neurological baseline within one hour.
- History of febrile seizures and no history of afebrile seizures.
- Single seizure associated with current episode of febrile illness.
- No diagnosed progressive neurological conditions.
- No diagnosed developmental delay.

Ibuprofen.text: Tablets: 20-40kg: 200mg PO (liquid): Ibuprofen 100mg/5ml  3-6 months (6-8 kg): 3 - 4ml 6-12 months (8-10 kg): 4 - 5ml 1-3 years (10-14 kg): 5 - 7ml 3-5 years (14-18 kg): 7 - 9ml 5-7 years (18-22 kg): 9 - 11ml 7-9 years (22-28 kg): 11 - 14ml 9-12 years (28-40 kg): 14 - 20ml

##### Type
Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory

##### Presentations
200mg tablet
 100mg/5ml liquid 

##### Actions

- Analgesic

- Anti-pyretic

- Anti-inflammatory

- Inhibits prostaglandin synthesis via inhibition of COX-1 and COX-2

#### Uses
Analgesia for mild-moderate pain due to inflammation

#### Adverse Effects

- Nausea

- Dyspepsia 

- GI ulceration or bleeding

- Diarrhoea

- Headache

- Dizziness

#### Contraindications

- Known hypersensitivity to NSAIDs

- Acute asthma

- Dehydration

- Current peptic ulcers

- Known or suspected active bleeding

- Inability to swallow/impaired gag reflex

- End stage renal or hepatic failure

- Acute heart failure

- Previous ibuprofen administration within 4 hours

#### Precautions

- History of peptic ulcers or GI bleeding

- Hepatic impairment

- Asthma

- Cardiovascular disease

#### Pregnancy and Breastfeeding##### Pregnancy
- **Contraindicated**

##### Breastfeeding
- Safe to use

#### Doses##### Adult
- ≥40kg: 400mg
- ≤40kg: 200mg
##### Paediatric
- Tablets: 20-40kg: 200mg
- PO (liquid): Ibuprofen 100mg/5ml 
- 3-6 months (6-8 kg): 3 - 4ml
- 6-12 months (8-10 kg): 4 - 5ml- 1-3 years (10-14 kg): 5 - 7ml- 3-5 years (14-18 kg): 7 - 9ml- 5-7 years (18-22 kg): 9 - 11ml- 7-9 years (22-28 kg): 11 - 14ml- 9-12 years (28-40 kg): 14 - 20ml

Paracetamol.text: Previous paracetamol dose in last 4 hours (oral doses only) Previous paracetamol dose in last 6 hours (IV doses only)
Do not exceed 60mg/kg/24hours. Children aged 10-12 may take a 500mg tablet if preferred.

##### Type
Simple analgesic / antipyretic 

##### Presentations
**PO:** 48mg/ml colour free liquid (oral) 
**IV:** 1g in 100ml in collapsible plastic flask (IV) 
**PO:** 500mg tablet (oral) 

##### Actions

- Antipyretic

- Mild—moderate analgesic

Onset Oral: 30 minutes 
 IV: 5-10 minutes 

#### Uses

- Pyrexia in children who have had or may have a febrile convulsion (for ACTAS treatment, pyrexia is generally >38\xb0C)

- Mild to moderate pain (oral)

- Moderate to severe pain (IV)

#### Adverse Effects
Rare – none of these side effects have been confirmed or refuted as being linked to the casual use of paracetamol:

- Dyspepsia

- Nausea

- Allergic reactions

- Haematological reactions

- Dizziness

- Pain at injection site

#### Contraindications

- Known or suspected allergy to paracetamol

- Previous paracetamol dose in last 4 hours (oral doses only)

- Previous paracetamol dose in last 6 hours (IV doses only)

- Children who do not have a sufficient gag reflex to swallow the measured dose 

- Not to be given to children
